title: Quantum--Fluid Correspondence for Systems of Nonrelativistic Spin-$\frac{1}{2}$ Particles
abstract: We show that a charged fluid endowed with an internal spin degree of freedom naturally satisfies the Pauli equation for a nonrelativistic spin-1/2 particle, and that a collection of n such interacting fluids can be reformulated as an Euler flow in 3n dimensions, thereby providing a natural representation of a system of n Pauli particles. These results provide a fluid-mechanical derivation of the Pauli equation and extend the Madelung, or quantum-hydrodynamic, picture to many-particle quantum systems. In particular, they imply that an n-qubit quantum computer can, at least in principle, be realized as a suitable combination of n fluids, or equivalently as a 3n-dimensional Euler flow.
summary: This paper establishes a formal mathematical equivalence between the Pauli equation for spin-1/2 particles and the hydrodynamic equations of a fluid with spin. It demonstrates that many-particle quantum systems can be mapped to higher-dimensional fluid flows, suggesting that fluid-mechanical systems could theoretically implement quantum computation.
main_problem: Establishing a mathematical correspondence between the dynamics of nonrelativistic spin-1/2 particles (the Pauli equation) and the hydrodynamic evolution of a charged fluid with internal spin.
main_result: The authors prove that a fluid with spin obeys the hydrodynamic Madelung form of the Pauli equation and that a system of N interacting particles can be represented as a single 3N-dimensional Euler flow, implying a computational duality between fluids and quantum computers.
method: The study employs the Madelung transform, Clebsch potentials, and Hamiltonian formalism (noncanonical Poisson brackets) to derive hydrodynamic equations from fluid-mechanical principles and to prove the propagation of the correspondence.
model_or_system: A system of nonrelativistic spin-1/2 particles governed by the Pauli Hamiltonian, and its hydrodynamic counterpart, a charged fluid endowed with an internal spin degree of freedom.
key_observables: Fluid density (rho), velocity (u), and spin field (s).
important_parameters: Nonrelativistic regime, magnetoquasistatic approximation, and a scale separation between fast spin dynamics and slow fluid dynamics.
main_assumptions: Non-zero density, non-vanishing canonical vorticity, and the assumption of smallness in helicity density and spin variations.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the mapping from Pauli wavefunctions to hydrodynamic variables and the converse view of N particles as N interacting fluids. Figure 2 illustrates the commutation of the Madelung transform with the evolution of the Pauli and hydrodynamic systems.
paper_structure: The paper begins by establishing the mapping from spinors to hydrodynamic variables, proceeds to derive the hydrodynamic Pauli equation from fluid principles using scale separation, and concludes by demonstrating the Hamiltonian structure and the N-particle to 3N-dimensional fluid mapping.
why_interesting: It suggests a profound theoretical duality where fluid dynamics can serve as a proxy for quantum evolution, potentially allowing for the use of fluid systems to simulate quantum computers or vice versa.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Schematic illustration of the quantum–ﬂuid correspondence developed in the present work. Panel (a) shows the usual Pauli wavefunction description and its Madelung transform into the hydrodynamic vari- ables (ρ, u, s), consisting of the density, velocity, and spin ﬁelds. Panel (b) represents the corresponding hydrodynamic system, whose governing equations are those of the Madelung form of the Pauli equation. Panel (c) summarizes the converse viewpoint explored in this paper: a quantum particle may be modeled as a ﬂuid with spin, and a system of N Pauli particles may be represented by N interacting ﬂuids with spin, or equivalently by a higher-dimensional Madelung plasma.
